candidates.name: Colleen Melody
candidates.prior_practice: Civil Rights Division Chief, WA Attorney General's Office. Career civil rights enforcement attorney, ~20 years.
candidates.endorsements: Gov. Ferguson; sitting WA Supreme Court justices; King County Bar.

signals.text: Career civil rights litigator; campaign materials emphasize equity and access to justice. No published textualist or originalist self-identification.
Cited admiration for U.S. Justice Elena Kagan in interviews and judicial discussion materials.
Selected by Gov. Ferguson, who as Attorney General defended Washington's capital gains tax through the Quinn v. State litigation.
No reported direct opinions on Art. VII §1 or Culliton; no published tax-law writings located.

questions: How would you approach a precedent that is widely regarded as legally dubious but has decades of reliance interests behind it?
What weight should the Washington Supreme Court give to U.S. Supreme Court stare decisis frameworks when interpreting the state constitution?

candidates.name: Scott Edwards
candidates.current_role: Partner, Ballard Spahr LLP (Seattle). Affiliate faculty, UW School of Law.
candidates.prior_practice: State and local tax controversy practice. Represents taxpayers in Washington Department of Revenue and Board of Tax Appeals disputes.

signals.text: Spent his career on the taxpayer side of Washington tax controversies, including matters involving classification of taxes under Art. VII.
Campaign materials emphasize 'rule of law,' tax-system fairness, and predictability for taxpayers.
Affiliate faculty at UW Law where he teaches state and local tax — exposure to Art. VII §1 doctrine is professional, not incidental.
Authored or co-authored tax bar publications on Washington tax structure. No reported public position on Culliton specifically.

questions: As a tax practitioner, how should the court evaluate whether to revisit a tax-doctrine precedent that has shaped 90 years of revenue policy?
What is the proper role of the judiciary when the legislature enacts a tax whose constitutional status is contested?

candidates.name: J. Michael Diaz
candidates.prior_practice: Judge, WA Court of Appeals (Division One). Former Assistant United States Attorney; DOJ Civil Rights Division.
candidates.endorsements: Sitting WA Supreme Court justices; bar association ratings strong.

signals.text: Inslee appointee. Inslee signed the capital gains tax into law and his administration defended it in Quinn v. State.
Federal-prosecutor and DOJ Civil Rights background. Appellate opinions tend toward careful statutory construction with attention to legislative intent.
Public writings emphasize institutional credibility of the courts, plain meaning, and clear analytical reasoning.
No reported direct Court of Appeals opinion on Art. VII §1 or Culliton. Federal background means limited state-constitutional tax exposure.

questions: How does federal stare decisis doctrine inform your approach to long-standing Washington Supreme Court precedent?
When the constitutional text and a long-standing interpretation diverge, what should govern?

candidates.name: David Stevens
candidates.prior_practice: General-jurisdiction trial judge. Prior private practice in Mason County.
candidates.endorsements: WAGOP recommendation (2026); local bar and law-enforcement endorsements.

signals.text: Public statements cite Justices Scalia and Alito as influences and emphasize 'constitution as written' textualism.
Strict-textualist reading of Art. VII §1 — that 'property' includes income because the 1933 court said so — entrenches Culliton rather than overturning it.
Aligned with conservative judicial-recommendation infrastructure; backed by Republican party recommendation.
No direct appellate opinions on Art. VII (superior court is trial level). Position derived from stated methodology, not record.

questions: If the original public meaning of 'property' in 1889 differed from the 1933 court's construction, which should control?
How should a textualist approach a precedent that has shaped tax policy for nine decades?

candidates.name: Ian Birk
candidates.current_role: Judge, WA Court of Appeals (Division One)
candidates.prior_practice: Plaintiff-side civil litigation, Seattle. Constitutional and civil-rights litigation experience.
candidates.endorsements: Broad slate of progressive elected officials and labor organizations.

signals.text: Inslee appointee to the Court of Appeals — same governor who signed the capital gains tax.
Plaintiff-side civil litigator before the bench; familiar with constitutional litigation strategy.
Endorsement slate skews heavily toward progressive elected officials and labor organizations relative to other Position 4 candidates.
No reported Court of Appeals opinion squarely addressing Art. VII §1 or Culliton.

questions: What is the proper role of stare decisis when a precedent significantly constrains the legislature's revenue authority?
How do you distinguish between fixing a doctrinal error and judicial policymaking?

candidates.name: Sean O'Donnell
candidates.prior_practice: Former King County Deputy Prosecuting Attorney. Long career in criminal trial work prior to the bench.
candidates.endorsements: Cross-partisan endorsements from law enforcement, prosecutors, and bar associations.

signals.text: Court-administration and trial-judge profile. No public ideological signature on constitutional methodology.
Career prosecutor before the bench; trial-court orientation rather than appellate or doctrinal.
Cross-partisan endorsements typical of moderate King County judicial candidates.
No reported public position on Art. VII or Culliton. Superior court bench provides no Art. VII opinion record.

questions: How would a trial-court background inform how you weigh reliance interests when considering overruling precedent?
What kinds of cases do you believe the court should be most cautious about reaching?

candidates.name: David Shelvey
candidates.fundraising: PDC reports $0 raised as of latest filing.

signals.text: Filed but appears to have no campaign infrastructure, no fundraising, and no public endorsements. Included for completeness.
No public statements on judicial methodology located.
None located.

questions: Why are you seeking this seat, and what experience qualifies you to serve on the state's highest court?

candidates.name: Theo Angelis
candidates.current_role: Justice, WA Supreme Court (sworn in April 2026)
candidates.prior_practice: K&L Gates (Seattle), 26 years. Appellate practice leader, later global IP lead. Constitutional, appellate, tribal, real property, IP litigation.
candidates.endorsements: Sitting WA Supreme Court justices; Gov. Ferguson; former Gov. Gregoire; Democratic officeholders.

signals.text: Ferguson appointment. As AG, Ferguson defended capital gains tax in Quinn.
Long-tenure appellate and constitutional litigator; treatise contributor on Washington law.
Campaign language emphasizes 'careful legal analysis and thoughtful decision-making' — moderate, institutionalist tone rather than textualist or originalist.
No public tax, Art. VII, or takings record located.

questions: How does appellate-practice experience shape your approach to stare decisis?
What weight do you give to legislative responses when the court interprets revenue authority?

candidates.name: David Larson
candidates.prior_practice: Municipal court bench; prior private practice. Repeat candidate for higher judicial office.
candidates.endorsements: Conservative-leaning endorsements; previously backed by Project 42 in prior judicial races.

signals.text: Self-described textualist; public statements invoke 'constitution as written' framing.
Previously supported by Project 42, an anti-income-tax-aligned independent expenditure organization, in prior runs.
Anti-income-tax candidates have a strong incentive to preserve Culliton because it operates as the constitutional shield against a graduated income tax.
No appellate opinions (municipal-court bench). Position inferred from stated methodology and funding network.

questions: If you concluded that Culliton was wrongly decided as an original matter, what would your approach be?
How should the court treat reliance interests when those interests are held primarily by state government rather than private parties?

candidates.name: Sharonda Amamilo
candidates.prior_practice: Former public defender. Trial-court bench experience.
candidates.endorsements: Seeking Democratic LD endorsements; bar-association ratings pending.

signals.text: Public-defender career suggests left-of-center civil-liberties orientation.
Stated stare decisis framing appears more conservative-formal than reformist.
Pursuing Democratic-aligned endorsements but with a procedural-justice rather than tax-policy frame.
No tax or Art. VII record located. Genuine uncertainty on Culliton posture.

questions: What four or five factors most heavily weigh in your stare decisis analysis?
Does a precedent's reliance interest weigh differently when the relying party is the legislature itself?

candidates.name: Debra Stephens
candidates.appointed_by: Gov. Christine Gregoire (initially appointed 2008); elected since.
candidates.prior_practice: Court of Appeals (Division Three); academic and appellate-litigation background.
candidates.endorsements: Broad bipartisan judicial and bar-association endorsements typical of an incumbent Chief Justice.

signals.text: Authored the lead opinion in Quinn v. State (2023) upholding the capital gains tax — and expressly declined to revisit Culliton, instead characterizing the capital gains tax as an excise rather than property tax.
Institutionalist. Quinn relied on the excise-tax framework rather than overruling 90-year-old precedent.
Public writings and CLE talks emphasize careful statutory construction, judicial restraint, and stare decisis.

If a graduated income tax styled as a tax on income reached the court, Stephens has signaled she would not be the vote to overrule Culliton outright — though she may permit alternative characterizations.

questions: When the Quinn opinion declined to revisit Culliton, what considerations drove that choice?
Under what circumstances would the court be justified in overruling a tax-doctrine precedent of comparable vintage?
